text: The gallbladder is visible, with the fundus grasped by tooth forceps and pushed back to facilitate exposure.
The surgeon separates flimsy fibrous adhesions between the duodenum and the gallbladder, pushing the gallbladder upward and into clearer view.
The duodenum is visible, having been safely repositioned downward, allowing for better exposure of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a Hartman's pouch grasper to manipulate the gallbladder, providing better exposure for the ongoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Three forceps are used: one holds the fundus of the gallbladder and pushes it back, while another manipulates Hartman's pouch at the lower portion of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a curved Maryland Dissector to remove adhesions and peritoneum, carefully dissecting from both sides of the gallbladder.

The surgeon identifies a round lymph node on the right side and proceeds to the left or anterior side, approaching the cystic duct. The common bile duct (CBD) is clearly visible below as the surgeon prepares to perform dissection in this area.
The surgeon performs dissection at the posterior calyx angle between the gallbladder and the cystic duct, creating a visible window in the tissue.
The surgeon performs a classical posterior calyx angle dissection between the gallbladder and cystic duct, creating a window in the tissue.
The surgeon combines posterior dissection with anterior dissection around the gallbladder and cystic duct, revealing anatomical structures such as the common bile duct and lymph node.
The surgeon peels away fibro fatty tissue and fascia from the surgical field, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The gallbladder is visible, showing its connection with the common hepatic duct and the common bile duct.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery supplying the gallbladder, noting its division into two branches.
The surgeon has torn a lymph node during the dissection process, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the lymph node of Lund, located near the gallbladder and bile ducts.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, noting its position as an indicator of the main artery supplying blood to the gallbladder. The artery is visibly pulsating as it pushes blood towards the gallbladder, and preparations are made to block this vessel before removing the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully delineates the posterior branch of the cystic artery to prevent tearing and bleeding. The cystic duct is visible, connecting the gallbladder to the common bile duct, as the surgeon removes blood clots from the area.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic artery and cystic duct from both posterior and anterior perspectives, ensuring precise identification of these structures for safe dissection.
The surgeon exposes the fossa or bed of the gallbladder, showing its position lying directly on the liver surface.
The surgeon clears the lower one-third portion of the cystic duct, ensuring visibility of the anatomical structures involved in the cholestectomy procedure.
The common bile duct is clearly visible below the surgical field, providing a clear view for the ongoing procedure.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, cystic duct, and cystic artery, proceeding cautiously to avoid damage to these structures.
The surgeon prepares to clip and cut the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring a clear view of both the anterior and posterior branches of the cystic artery.

The surgeon encloses the posterior branch of the cystic artery within the clip, ensuring the tip of the clip is visible. The handle is pressed firmly to secure the clip in place, preventing it from slipping.
The surgeon attempts to further delineate the cystic duct and cystic artery, ensuring clear visualization of these structures in relation to the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a clip applicator to further clear the area around the cystic duct and artery, preparing to apply a clip above these structures.
The surgeon clears the surgical field around the cystic artery, ensuring visibility for the application of clips.
The surgeon loads a second clip into the clip applicator and applies it to both branches of the cystic artery, ensuring secure placement.
The surgeon gently encloses the posterior or right side branch of the cystic artery within a surgical clip using a clip applicator.
The tip of the clip applicator is visible as the surgeon prepares to apply a clip to the cystic artery branches.

The surgeon applies two clips on the cystic artery, one proximally and one distally, using a clip applicator. The area is cleared for visibility, and the artery is cut without applying additional clips distally.
The surgeon applies a clip onto the cystic duct, positioned slightly above the junction with the common bile duct, securing the duct in preparation for further dissection.

The surgeon applies a second clip on the cystic artery, positioning it further away, and then places a distal clip on the cystic artery. The surgeon proceeds to cut between the distal clip and the second proximal clip.
The surgeon applies a clip on the distal side of the gallbladder, ensuring secure closure to prevent any potential backflow or spillover of bile.
The main branch of the cystic artery is visibly cut after the application of clips, as part of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses one side of the scissors to gently push away fibrofasci tissue, delineating the posterior branch of the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery hook to carefully remove the peritoneal connection of the gallbladder from the liver, while observing the pulsation in the cystic artery and checking the area around the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses a hooked monopolar cautery device to gently push under the fibrofascia tissue and peritoneum, carefully separating these layers without applying much pressure, ensuring the gallbladder tissue remains intact.
The surgeon is nearing the completion of the cholecystectomy, carefully separating the gallbladder completely from the liver bed.
